text: The surgeon has cut congenital adhesions and freed the colon, with the sigmoid colon and small bowel now separated. An incision is made in the recto-vesical pouch of the peritoneum, and both vas deferens and seminal vesicles are dissected. Straight needle sutures are placed through the bladder, looping around the seminal vesicles and vas deferens to keep them out of view during posterior dissection. The surgeon proceeds to dissect the recto-vesical pouch, cutting the Denonvilliers' fascia and positioning the rectum downward, while planning to extend the dissection laterally up to the levator muscles.
The surgeon is dissecting the lateral pedicle on the right side, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon removes the stitches securing the seminal vesicles, pulling the bladder to expose the prostate-vesicle junction for further dissection.

The surgeon defines the lateral boundaries of the prostate and proceeds medially, revealing the bladder fibers adjacent to the prostate and the urethra at the prostate-vesicle junction. The section appears oozy, indicating minor bleeding.

The surgeon addresses a part of the lateral pedicle that has not been fully dissected, using suction to manage minor bleeding in the area. The dissection is slightly oozy, but the bleeding is expected to stop shortly.
The surgeon identifies the fascia and neovascular bundle, further dissecting the area. A surgical clip is applied to secure the tissue. The intra-abdominal pressure is reduced to twelve mmHg.

The surgeon cuts at the bladder neck, carefully manipulating the tissue to open it. The bladder neck is partially sacrificed, ensuring the incision accommodates the presence of a large median lobe or post-TURP condition.
The surgeon completes lateral dissection on both sides, focusing on intrafascial planes, with visible separation of tissue layers.
The surgeon uses a 30-degree scope to visualize the anterior wall of the bladder and the urethra or bladder neck. A stitch is placed on the bladder neck as a marker.

The surgeon requests a two-zero micron suture and prepares the thirty-degree scope. A stitch is placed at the bladder neck to serve as a traction point and a marker for orientation during anastomosis, indicating the six o'clock position as the anastomosis begins at twelve o'clock.
The surgeon adjusts the endoscopic view to a thirty-degree angle, enhancing the visibility of the surgical field. The catheter is then removed, revealing a well-defined anatomical plane.
The surgeon navigates around the anterior wall of the urethra at the urethra-prostate junction, preparing to cut this area as part of the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully avoids the centorhinal plexus while performing cold dissection, opting not to use diathermy unless an arterial bleeder is visible.
The surgeon approaches the apical portion of the neurospinal bundle, preparing to dissect this area with careful technique.

The surgeon visually identifies the urethra during the prostatectomy procedure. The dorsal venous complex (DVC) remains unopened, and the surgeon discusses using a 30 cm V-lock suture for closing the DVC and performing anastomosis if the centaurinary plexus is opened.
The surgeon places a single suture at the six o'clock position for anastomosis, swapping instruments including the hot shear and needle holder to proceed with the procedure.
The surgeon applies a traction suture to the bladder using a straight needle, positioned just above the symphysis pubis, with the suture visibly passing through the bladder tissue.
The surgeon begins placing a suture at the six o'clock position, ensuring to include the mucosa in the stitch.
The surgeon inserts a Foley catheter into the patient's urethra, ensuring proper placement and securing it in position.
The surgeon observes the catheter being inserted and advancing smoothly into the bladder during the prostatectomy procedure.
The surgeon prepares to perform saline distension by filling the bladder, demonstrating the process visually.
